lesson_title: Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: To declare a pointer to a pointer to int: {{c1::int** ptrptr;}}
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.back: A pointer that holds the address of another pointer
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: **ptrptr dereferences twice: first to get ptr, then to get value. *ptrptr gives the address in ptr, **ptrptr gives the value (5).
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: An array of 10 pointers to int: {{c1::int* array[10];}} (read as "array of 10 pointer-to-int")
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.front: What are common uses for pointers to pointers?
cards.back:
• Dynamically allocated 2D arrays (array of pointers to arrays)
  • Allowing a function to modify a pointer parameter
  • C-style strings array (char** argv in main)
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text:
To create a dynamic 2D array (10x5):
{{c1::int** array{ new int*[10] };}}
for (int i{0}; i < 10; ++i)
    {{c2::array[i] = new int[5];}}
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.question: What's the correct way to delete this 2D array?
cards.answer:
Delete in reverse order:
for (int i{0}; i < 10; ++i)
    delete[] array[i];
delete[] array;
cards.explanation: Must delete each row first (delete[] array[i]), then delete the array of pointers (delete[] array). Reverse of allocation order.
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.concept: Problems with dynamic 2D arrays using pointers
cards.explanation:
Issues with int** 2D arrays:
  • Rows can be different lengths (not contiguous in memory)
  • Poor cache performance (not memory-contiguous)
  • Complex allocation and deallocation
  • Easy to create memory leaks
  • Must track dimensions separately
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.front: What is a flattened array and why use it for 2D data?
cards.back:
A flattened array stores 2D data in a 1D array: int* array = new int[rows*cols];
Access with: array[row*cols + col]

Benefits: contiguous memory, better cache performance, simpler allocation/deallocation
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: For a flattened 2D array with dimensions [rows][cols], access element at [row][col] using: array[{{c1::row * cols + col}}]
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.question: What's the difference between these two declarations?
cards.answer:
ptr1: pointer to pointer to int (can point to dynamic 2D array)
ptr2: array of 10 pointers to int (fixed-size array of pointers)
cards.explanation: ptr1 is a pointer (needs allocation), ptr2 is an array that contains 10 pointers (array itself is fixed, but each pointer can point to dynamic memory).
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.explanation:
Prefer std::vector over manual pointer management:
  • std::vector<std::vector<int>> for variable-sized 2D
  • Automatic memory management
  • No manual allocation/deallocation
  • Bounds checking available
  • Exception safe
Manual pointers are advanced and error-prone.
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: Given int** ptrptr, dereferencing once {{c1::*ptrptr}} gives a {{c2::pointer to int}}, dereferencing twice {{c3::**ptrptr}} gives the {{c4::int value}}.
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: To delete a 2D dynamic array, delete {{c1::rows first}} (delete[] array[i] in loop), then delete {{c2::the array of pointers}} (delete[] array).
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays

cards.text: Flattened 2D arrays use formula {{c1::row * cols + col}} to access elements and provide {{c2::better cache performance}} than pointer-to-pointer arrays.
cards.source: 19.4 - Pointers to pointers and dynamic multidimensional arrays
